topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. Dr. Albright was resigning. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly. All the late nights spent poring over his obscure theories, the encouraging nods during office hours—it all felt suddenly fragile, about to shatter. I started pacing my cramped dorm room, the worn carpet muffling the sound of my hurried footsteps. Every shadow in the room seemed to stretch and deepen, transforming the familiar furniture into looming shapes. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened computer screen, and for a moment, I couldn't quite recognize the person staring back. A tremor ran through my hands as I reached for my notebook, the pen feeling impossibly heavy, refusing to write anything coherent.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed to ask, the sound of my own voice thin and reedy in the echoing silence of the faculty lounge. Professor Davies didn't look up from his lukewarm coffee, his gaze fixed somewhere beyond the chipped Formica table. His silence was a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. The news had hit me like a sudden drop in temperature. My palms began to sweat, a clammy sheen spreading across my skin. I fiddled incessantly with the frayed edge of my sweater sleeve, wishing I could melt into the cheap upholstery. Every casual comment about the department felt loaded now, every shared glance loaded with unspoken farewells. I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles turned white, fighting the urge to bolt from the room altogether.

[story 3]

The campus library felt cavernous and strangely empty that afternoon. I was supposed to be reviewing my literature review, but the words swam on the page, unreadable. When I finally found the notification—a terse memo about Dr. Albright’s departure—my breath hitched. My heart began hammering against my sternum, a frantic, uneven rhythm I couldn't control. I stood by the tall, arched window, watching the distant quad. The sunlight felt harsh, exposing every vulnerability. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the solid reality of the building, but the world outside seemed too vast, too indifferent. I slowly turned away from the window, the movement stiff, as if moving required immense, draining effort.
